reviews.review_text:
Valheim's Core gameplay is fun but the game quickly falls apart once you realize almost every single mechanic is designed to slow you down and inflate playtime to an enormous degree, and while it's not TERRIBLE in multiplayer it's especially noticeable on repeat playthroughs and when playing solo. 

That being said, This wouldn't be a bad thing if any of the mechanics or biomes had depth but...they don't, at all. after YEARS of early access they still decide that extending the end game content is the top priority when ship sailing, dungeon delving, and exploration have BARELY any mechanical depth and are simply unfun once you've discovered everything once. 

The community definitely has something to do with my distain as the amount of "Game actually has X hours of content" comments irritate me because they always insinuate this game is masterfully crafted and infinity repayable when in reality most of what causes the massive playtimes are what I stated earlier. 

Most of all It irritates me that they are obviously stretching out development as long as possible and using the excuse of "we want everything to be meaningful and ready for release" when there is essentially no output that's supporting this AT ALL. the initial roadmap was super promising and essentially listed out things that would fix all my issues with the game and instead they scrapped it and went to this system of one major "content" update every 1 1/2 years that just slaps another biome at the end of the progression chain that the VAST majority of players wont reach because the games issues and lack of depth  becomes apparent before they even REACH the mist-lands or ash-lands. 

Its especially tragic because almost EVERYBODY thought this would be THE survival game when it first released. So again LET ME BE CLEAR I'm not saying the game is BAD by any means, quite the contrary. the music, art, and boss design are what made it stand out in the first place, but if you're expecting ANYTHING with staying power or depth that will be fun past 10-40 hours you'll be sorely disappointed, VERY fun with mods though
You are never safe in this game. You think you are geared up and skilled, until you get onetapped by fighter jet mosquito. Also you can have a dog.... 10 out of 10 game. Love it

5 years, half a decade in EA and we've recieved 2 biomes, a few materials,  a revamped food system and a magic system thats gated off for half the game. you canot defeat the 6th boss solo without cheesing the fight. leaving the fight to repair gear and re entering is expected, thats terrible game design.  if you get killed in the next region before establishing a portal, guess what. you need to build another resource intensive boat again. this game does not respect your time.

Valheim genuinely surprised me. I'm not a huge fan of open world survival crafting games. They're not really my thing and I don't find much enjoyment out of them, the only exception to me being Terraria. You can add Valheim to that list.

The game is very slow paced. But slow paced in a non-detrimental way I think. I'm only a mere 23 hours in and basically just now started crafting Bronze-level gear, if you want to know how truly "early" I am in this game. I can only realistically survive at the moment in the Meadows & Black Forest biomes, making the very, very first preparations to head into the Swamp next. The game's slow pace heavily encourages you to just simply explore your surroundings. Find abandoned villages or bases, make them your own. Slip into a dungeon or two. Kill some deer & boar, start with the basics, that sort of thing.

But there's genuinely one mechanic that makes this game far less punishing than say...Minecraft. And one I deeply appreciate, being that repairing your tools is free, provided you have the correct crafting station & level, and your tools don't disappear from your inventory when they break, meaning you can just repair them & keep going. You have no idea how much a simple part like this makes me want to stick around because I can just go back to my base, fix my items and keep going as if nothing ever happened. The one thing that genuinely makes me dislike open world survival crafting games is how punishing they are & how much micromanagement they require of the player. I get those who like that sort of thing, but it's usually just not for me.

The environmental design in this game is also gorgeous. Each biome is distinct in its own unique way, from the enemies you face, resources you can find & just the general atmosphere you find yourself in. I absolutely love the pixel-based style that this game has. It has a charm that scratches that PlayStation 1-era itch, so to speak. The soundtrack - atmospheric & relaxing most of the time. And this game has a Lithuanian translation (albeit not fully complete), which is also super, super cool. Very rare to see something like that in games and I appreciate it immensely.

The fact that the development team also took their time in optimizing the game, as well as making a native Linux version is an insanely huge plus. Linux gamers like me have a genuinely great port of the game to enjoy.

Even in early access, the game feels very complete. I'd say it's worth the price tag. I'm not sure how much the price will increase when it leaves early access, but even then I'd still say this game is worth it.

If you have 1 or 2 friends to play with, the game becomes even better as a social game. But even in solo I think you'll find plenty to enjoy here. If someone like me, who doesn't even like open world survival crafting games, can find genuine enjoyment and value in this game, I think you can too.

Solid recommendation from me as a great and fun game.

At this point, I have to give this a thumbs down While I love this game, the developers are lazy, the mods and community have carried this game in its Early Access. Iron Gate remails incompetent, just lazy. In the time this game has been in just early access, 5 years you can see so many other games and peer level developers relapsing updates and full fledged finished games. I beleive Valheim released and the developer wasn't ready and didn't have a clear vision. As of this review, Ashland's was released 2 years ago and there have been no content updates since then, since 2021 basically they added 2 biomes and a few cosmetics. Many he game continues to lack in features and time schedule. The game is largely unfinished without mods and I know, the development team has pocketed the money, sits and talks instead of walks.

This game is literally rubbish. Unless you enjoy building and getting triggered by the stupid character/biome and linear progression system, then this game is not for you.

If you like building and that sort of thing, it can be fun. However, the best building materials are only available in the end-game stage (the final biome in the current update). You have to progress all the way to the end game just to get the best building materials, and after that, I guess you just wait for the next update. Sailing and fishing are boring as hell. The fishing mechanics do not make any sense at all.

One example that annoyed me the most was spending hours gathering materials and sailing them to homebase to craft and upgrade your equipment, thinking that more armour means better defence/tanking, only to find out that blocking and parrying depend on your maximum health instead of your shield’s upgrade level or armour level. I died so many times in one specific biome that I lost a lot of skill levels, which made progressing all over again really annoying. (In this game, you lose skill levels every time you die, for example: Bow, Blocking, Sword, etc.)

Multiplayer is neither optimised nor stable at all, even though the game has been out for ages. (which is really sad since the game is not really that fun to play solo either.)

(World Modifiers can adjust the world difficulty settings to your liking, but apparently for a first playthrough it is best to keep them on the default settings.)

The community is fantastic, though. I got to meet some nice people on the official discord server. 

I could not finish the game, so I decided to delete my world to make sure I don't return and play other games that actually make sense.

a great game full of countless bugs and glitches to find by me, including crashing the game and accidentally duping some items! floating stones if you break their supports! a bed filled with beams, use them as a blanket! a bed right under a roof, sleep with your head sticking out gazing at the stars, with your toes warm on the fire! /rest while on a fire, a cozy demise! asphyxiation is a man's best friend!!!

Love the graphics and music and world and lore.  Gameplay mechanics and combat are fun and unique. Building is quite well programmed but a little tedious because of inventory/weight limitations.  

Was introduced to the game by a friend and used the mod launcher to improve the game.  But I don't really like modding myself, so I can't play anymore going back to vanilla with no extra armor slots, no quick food slots, no extra arrow slots... and whatever other tiny QoL improvements my friend insisted on modding in.  Game makers should always inherit the best mods from the community to improve their games.  Skyrim better menus, I'm looking at you.

You see my playtime. I’ve spent a decent amount of time playing Valheim with friends. And I will continue playing it until we’ve seen all content. It’s not a _bad_ game. But it’s not a good game, either. Since Valheim is still in Early Access at the time of writing, this review serves a double role of explaining why I wouldn’t recommend it, and providing feedback to the dev. There is no real order or logic to what will follow.

The tl;dr of this review is that Valheim introduces friction into every action. You have a goal you want to reach but Valheim gets in the way. That’s normal for games. Otherwise there would just be a ’you win’ button. Games are meant to have obstacles you need to overcome. But Valheim pricks you with a thousand needles every step of the way. That’s plain annoying. Many of these needle pricks come from cranking the realism slider up too high.

I would’ve liked to write more, but the character limit cuts this review short.

[h3]Enemies[/h3]

Especially in the early game, but also later, your best option – your only option to survive – is to run. Run as fast as the wind can carry you. But every single mob in the game is persistent like a bloodhound. When you’ve depleted and regained your stamina multiple times, they’re still after you. By that time, however, you’ve stumbled into the next enemy who will now also chase you. In the end you’ve crossed a whole ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ continent (the map in Valheim is composed of islands) just by running away from danger. Whatever it was you actually wanted to do at the beginning of that encounter, you’re now miles away from your target. Running away from mob after mob for minutes while you’re _trying_ to go in the opposite direction isn’t fun.

Another thing are the entry-level mobs. Throughout the game you tend to be in the overworld to farm some stuff – mowing down a forest for wood or mining a vein of copper. These things take time, but there’s also a meditative quality to spend a few hours (real-time) to eradicate a forest from the face of the earth… if it weren’t for the mobs. They’re often the very first enemies you find in the game, so they’re seriously underpowered by the time you need to farm this many resources.

What this effects to is that way too often you need to set down your actual task to one-hit some nuisances. In that stage of the game they don’t even deal damage because you’re so overpowered compared to them. But you still can’t ignore them because their attacks still have the knockback of a freight train. Seriously. Getting hit by a pebble somehow moves you 2 meters. So you _have_ to kill them.

There would be such an easy fix: Let me buy and equip an item that just shoos away boring enemies. Price it at 2k so it’s not immediately available. The item creates a bubble of, say, 50m radius that enemies whose attacks would hurt you by less than 10HP (accounting for your equipped armor) can’t penetrate.

[h3]The world is repetitive[/h3]

Valheim is a game of exploration. Its world is huge and procedurally generated. Unfortunately, it is boringly generated. There are multiple different biomes, and they are interesting. Each is completely different than the one before. But [i]within the same biome,[/i] everything is the same. And that’s a shame. If you’ve seen the biome once there is really no joy in finding the biome again.

At one point in our playthrough that became painfully obvious: For each biome there is a boss you need to fight in order to progress. However, not every [i]instance[/i] of the biome has the boss. For two biomes we had to spend literal IRL hours searching multiple different instances of these biomes until we found the boss. This reduces the gameplay to going to a new island, running across the whole thing as fast as you can and returning because it was empty after all. There is exploration in the sense that you fill in the map, but it’s dull. There is nothing interesting to find, nothing to grab your attention. Just a series of sprints over land that you’ve seen again and again. Running for hours over a barren landscape in order to progress isn’t fun.

[h3]Traversing the world is annoying[/h3]

As I’ve said the world is _huge_. To go to remote parts you build a ship and set sail… for a very long time. On our last long journey that I remember, _one trip_ took somewhere between 30 and 45 minutes. Crossing our known world at that time would’ve taken 60–90 minutes. Due to a game mechanic I won’t spoil here, you can go faster if enough people are on board.

Now here’s the WTF moment of this game: Only one person can steer the ship. [i]Everyone else on board has nothing to do![/i] They have no agency and no tasks. You [i]have[/i] to sail long distances, and it’s beneficial if many people are on board, but there’s nothing to do for them. The winning strategy for them is to go AFK and do something else. Now tell me that this isn’t bad game design. Even the person steering the ship has long stretches of time where there’s nothing to do. You just go in a straight line and that’s it.

Another annoyance that factors into the previous one, but is really a separate issue: The wind direction is random. In the early game that was fun. You’d have to wait for good winds before you could set sail and go exploring. But in the late game, when you need to cross kilometers and are thrown back to ruddering a good portion of the journey, you’ll just curse the game. It’s so boring.

In our little group we’ve turned towards just one person venturing out while the others do something better with their time. Again: A multiplayer game that punishes doing ♥♥♥♥ together. Make it make sense to me.

[hr][/hr]

There is one biome in particular that is just a pain in the ass from start to finish. I’m talking about [spoiler]the Mistlands[/spoiler]. Running and jumping in the game depletes stamina. You have to wailt for it to refill. In this particular biome you [i]must[/i] run [i]and[/i] jump [i]all the time[/i]. This leads to your ‘walking’ (let’s be gracious and call it that) for 20–50 meters and then wait for your stamina bar to fill up again. 30m more, wait, 50m, wait. I cannot for the life of me comprehend how anyone could opine that this is fun gameplay. At that point Valheim turns into an idle game.

Luckily this biome can be finished quickly (if you’re lucky with finding the boss). But that still accounts for several hours of gameplay. At the end of the biome our party unanimously voted this biome the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ and all of us were glad it was over. We won’t be returning unless we absolutely have to.

This has the side effect that we won’t explore one of the game’s mechanics, [spoiler]wizardry[/spoiler] because we can’t be arsed to farm the required resources. Dev, you wasted your time putting that in. Sorry.

[h3]The programming is bad.[/h3]

This is a big one that has caused a lot of frustration: Disconnects. Every session we play, we get multiple disconnects. I think that the internet connection of my peers might be bad. If the connection gets lost, there’s nothing Valheim can do. That’s on us. However, if Valheim decides to kick one player off the server while our shared voice chat keeps working uninterrupted, then I blame the game. And I also blame the game if that player can’t simply reconnect. If one player disconnects, they often have to close Valheim and open it again before they can join the server again. There’s really no excuse for that. Fix the game!

We’ve had brittle connections in other games. We were much more tolerant with those games because they made re-joining painless. Valheim doesn’t. Another punch in the guts is that Valheim fails to keep the game world in a consistent state. On multiple occasions we have lost valuable items because the connection broke as one player was transferring items to/from chests. Sometimes items get duplicated, but subjectively that doesn’t make up for losing items. Losing your progress to a bug isn’t fun.

This game makes you feel creative and adventurous. Exploring the world, building bases, and surviving are fun, especially when playing with friends. It encourages creativity, exploration, and teamwork, and every adventure feels rewarding. The game has a great balance of challenge and freedom, which keeps every session exciting and memorable. ❤︎

This game has a quarter million reviews already, there's not much to say that hasn't already been said.  This is my go-to forever game when all the rest of the gaming industry has been enshitified.  It's cheap, it's packed with content, there aren't any microtransactions.  Buy it.

I can't recommend this game, but you can play it with a lot of nuances.

Pros:

1) Interesting building system that gives a nice relaxing vibe.
2) Beautiful surroundings (at least for the first 1-2 hours, and only if you don’t have rain, fog, darkness, rainfog, darknessrain, snowdarknessfog etc).
3) Curious low-poly art style.

Cons:

1) Almost ZERO Quality-of-Life features. Playing without simple QoL mods feels absolutely disgusting.
2) Loot system is terrible -- no proper tools for sorting or comfortably managing your inventory.
3) Planting something as simple as carrots without mods wastes an enormous amount of time.
4) Crafting takes way too much time.
5) Transporting resources is extremely tedious.
6) The combat system is awful. There’s almost no stamina management, every move drains stamina, and if you’re not fighting on perfectly flat ground you lose even more stamina and can’t even kill basic mobs.
7) Play with range weapon, everything else just make you glasscanon, and you must grind more items for lose them again and again
8) The big procedurally generated world feels unnecessary. It’s boring -- the SAME biomes, SAME structures, SAME enemies everywhere.
9) Bosses are a massive disappointment. It’s not about skill, it’s just a huge time sink -- 10 hours of dealing damage and spamming RMB + Shift. Only the first boss gives any real emotion.
10) Enemy pathfinding is horrendous. For example: In the frost cave mobs get stuck in "air"walls and can't move, but then can hit you through REALwalls.

My recommendation if you still want to play:

1) Play in co-op.
2) Play with the ability to transport ore through portals (transporting resources once or twice is fine, 50+ times -- not fun at all).
3) Set minimum death penalty (farming XP for skills in late game is pure time-wasting).
4) Use mods for quick item sorting in chests. And the ability to pull items from nearby chests when creating something.
5) Honestly, you can just build a ship, sail for 2 hours, see everything the game has to offer, and uninstall.

Have fun.

[b] Valheim [/b] is a game I really don't understand when it comes to its popularity. It never fully clicked for me, and after more time with it, it felt more disappointing than engaging. I can see what it is trying to do, but the execution just didn't work for me.

For a game that has been in Early Access for a long time, it doesn't offer anything truly special. The world can feel empty and repetitive, and exploration quickly loses its appeal. After some time, everything starts to look very similar, which makes it hard to stay engaged.

The combat system also leaves a lot to be desired. Fights are not very satisfying, hitboxes can be inconsistent, and the balance between enemies and the player sometimes feels unfair. Even simpler enemies can be more annoying than challenging.

There are other survival games that do these systems better and feel more fresh. For me, Valheim is a big disappointment.

Never have I been so engaged and captivated by a game as much as Valheim, The atmosphere, the difficult but rewarding gameplay, the uniqueness of every world, the co-op saga, the base-building and the blend of all of these things made into a viking saga purgatory are something I have never experienced.  Hats off to the developers! Can't wait to see what else would come to the 10th world. 

P.S. - The beers are happy. So am I

The definition of a game that could be remarkable but ends up a terrible grindfest and wasting the player's time. 

Early game you can feel the joy of discovering a new world to explore and build on, but quickly you find out how much of it is repetition biome after biome. However, you can still enjoy many things: The world can be beautiful and some lore bits can be found it you are curious. Combat can feel rewarding with some mechanics (dodging and parrying) and different builds, but at the same time, so much is thrown at you that combat really becomes how many weird food ingredients you grinded for better food and potions since stamina regeneration remains the same the whole game and you roleplay as a fearsome Viking constantly running away from everything, praying the stamina goes up before death finds you again (and you lose a few levels of many skills that will take an hour to level up again). Boss battles at least can be fun if your favorite RNG favors you.

Managed to defeat Fader rushing the game with a friend in under 125h/a week with the teleport metal option activated (thankfully we have some sandbox options to enable after some protest) because the alternative was just distasteful considering all the wandering and moving already necessary to finish the game. Couldn't imagine how suckers defend it as the default way considering how buggy the navigation, storage and solo play is. Limiting portal usage just to waste your time, basically.  Building and farming are a test of patience that also just waste your time (you need to build a workbench to destroy things and cover it if you want to build things, really? Plant a single crop at a time with low yield after a long time, really?)

Mistlands and Ashlands were a lesson in annoying enemy behavior. Messy mechanics, constant spawns and grinding limited resources for the remainder of the game. From what I've read, the devs are mostly against necessary QoL changes that everyone asks for and content drops have been slow. Tragic.

Hell, I can't even imagine a parent or someone who works all the day long and has only one or two hours to play the game each day and the whole gaming session would be just sailing until they find something useful, farming or mining slowly every day to achieve barely any progress or just trying to get back your loot from your body from stupid, random deaths.

Despite all that, I will probably come back when the full game releases (someday, somehow HA) and if we get some awfully needed changes (and achievements become available).

Its a fun game but the development of it leaves me conflicted.

Its been 5 years instead of 1 to be completed. The raodmap changed but the upates are extremely slow, even for an indie studio. It feels like they made their money (which is great) are are now milking early access as long as possible. When I purchased the game I was expecting it to be finished within the 1 year as they advertised, but its been 5 years! The small updates that come out are not worth re-installing the game for. I have to leave a negative review until its finished as its just unacceptable.
Very good game, but my teammate Julo.... to už je iná liga, ako keby som videl hrať chatgpt

I wish they made more games like this with such graphics and cozy feeling. I love playing games that feels a little retro and fun to play at the same time. If the developers of this game made other games like Valheim with different myths or stories, I would absolutely paly it

This game is honestly amazing. I played it solo from start to finish and never got bored for a second. The open world, exploration, building system, and boss fights were all really fun. Building your own village and improving it over time feels super satisfying. The atmosphere, music, and overall survival experience are done really well too. It’s probably even more fun with friends, but even solo it’s incredibly addictive. Definitely a must-play if you like survival and building games.
amazing gameplay and experience, i can see there was alot of work into this game and gives a unique playthrough experience
Great game full of challenges and exploration. you can literally just explore for days and not get bored

Good game. Cozy. Fun with friends. Interfered with my college studies.

Normally I wouldn't have left a review, but my buddy left a "Not Recommended" review, so I'm just doing this to piss him off.
Like every negative review said, this game has too much tedium, every little thing costs way too much time..

this game is absolute perfection- it's beautiful, cozy, and challenging!! <3
if you're thinking about getting this game- JUST GET IT!! I PROMISE IT IS SM FUN!!!
Don't sleep on the jumping skill, it's crucial for the late game
Great game, a bit hard at first but really catch on as time goes on.

i never really understood the hype for this game. its slow and punishing, the progression and UI leave a lot to be desired. I'm tired of games determining your power level by what food you eat, and limiting one crucial resource you need to maintain your gear and weapons. I was enjoying Archery just to have my bow break and not be able to repair or make a new one without scouring the map for boars for an hour...
Crashed when I entered my first dungeon and then deleted the last few saves I had setting me back hours of progress. Not a big fan of that

Personally felt dying was too unfair for how often I felt enemies spawned out of nowhere, had portals set up which the AI target and break which only makes it so much worse, was having fun until that happen so take this with a grain of salt idk wouldn't recommend if you don't game a lot

:Includes massive swimming mechanic:

Fails to include a way of swimming DOWN, making you lose all your loot from ocean fights UNLESS you build a GIANT highdive that will force your character lower in the water.

Game breaking bug due to lazy ass devs.  I'm beginning to think they didn't choose to leave their old studio because they really wanted to make this game.  It's feeling like incompetence drove them out to make this.

Ridiculous grind fest. Took me 3 hours to gather, haul and smelt 4 times my carrying capacity in copper ore only to gets you 2 items out of it. I ain't doing that again.
The core of the game is very interesting and can be pleasant. I do enjoy the slower pace, but for the amount of time and ressources required for the smallest thing, I can't recommend.

Great game, painfully slow development. Go buy another game like Abiotic Factor or Enshrouded

Valheim itself is a very good game. The atmosphere, exploration, building, and early progression are genuinely excellent, that's why i have nearly 900 hours. That is what makes the development pace so frustrating. They really wasted their chance. 

I honestly wont be buying another game from Iron Gate. The amount of content added since launch feels incredibly small compared with other survival/crafting games and similar indie studios. When you look at games like Satisfactory, Abiotic Factor, Enshrouded, and others, the difference in pace and communication is hard to ignore.

For a Viking game where sailing is such a central part of the experience, cutting the Ocean biome update  feels just feels so off, that its even hard do describe. The game also still has large gaps in progression, with magic only appearing very late, and many basic QOL features are still missing. There is no proper recipe search, no good recipe sorting, and many systems still feel unnecessarily clunky after all this time.

Performance is also still not where it should be. Multiplayer servers can lag heavily once people start building larger bases, and the optimisation simply does not feel good enough for a game that has been in early access for this long.

Valheim has a fantastic foundation, but Iron Gate’s development pace is painfully slow.

Never have I felt so bored and frustrated playing this game. As you continue playing just to get more items, I started realizing that none of it really matters because the gameplay loop barely evolves. You grind materials, craft the next set of gear, beat a boss, then repeat the exact same process again with little variation or depth added along the way. After a while, progression stops feeling rewarding and instead feels like a chore stretched out by unnecessary farming and travel time.. What disappointed me most is how empty the experience began to feel after the initial wonder wore off. The world looks atmospheric at first, but exploration quickly loses its magic because there’s not much meaningful content waiting for you outside of resource gathering. Combat feels clunky and repetitive, building eventually becomes the main attraction, and if building isnt enough to carry the experience for you, the game starts feeling shallow. What makes this worse is that games like Enshrouded managed to keep me engaged far more despite releasing much later. It has more interesting progression, more varied activities, better movement, more rewarding exploration, and systems that actually feel modern and evolving. The fact that a newer game already feels more content-rich and engaging says a lot considering Valheim has been out for years. 

Initially, I really wanted to enjoy Valheim long-term because the atmosphere and concept are genuinely good, but after dozens of hours, it just felt repetitive, slow, and lacking in meaningful depth. Instead of feeling excited to continue, I mostly felt tired and unmotivated every time I open this game :l

This game is incredible. Its addicting, immersive, fun, and beautiful (in its own way). It spawned an entire genre of Valheim clones and was so close to being a masterpiece. In fact, its just one update away from being a masterpiece, but this thing has been in early access for almost 5 years. The core gameplay loop revolves around progressing to the next biome. They added the last biome 2 years and 3 days ago. 

The dev cycle here is just too damn long. They made a killing and got lazy. Don't buy this game unless you want to dump 100's of hours into a world just to find out that the game is incomplete and its literally 1 biome away from being finished.

Honestly, this game had a lot of potential, been coming back from time to time but not anymore. Updates are few and take a lot of time in between them for basically nothing. Modders are doing more work than the devs i would say.
Steer clear of this for now unless there is a major update

Before I start the review I need to give the context I have played about 60 hours on xbox app and my friend somehow convinced me to buy it again.

This game is insanely over rated unless you literally only care about cozy vibe and nice looks. The building seems to be nice but is crippled by. The main problems are the insane amounts of grind and the combat.

The game starts out fine in a meadows area where you can build some things and hunt some basic animals. Everything seems fine at this point. It is kind of fun here. You cook your food and build a basic house until you fight the first boss who is also ok. We then move onto the next biome and this is where the game starts to show what the rest is going to be like. You need to mine copper and tin to make bronze and it takes a LONG time. You get to watch your stupid little man lift up and drop his pickaxe again, and again, and again until you have too much weight to carry everything back. 
In valhiem you have a limited amount of weight you can carry and a limited amount of inventory space. There is NO WAY to increase inventory space and there are NO armor slots. A large amount of your space you have is taken up by your clothes and basic things you need. The game encourages you to carry food, tools, weapons, clothes and other situational stuff and there is nothing like a backpack in this game. As the game goes on more and more needed items are needed and I hear once you get to the really late game you have like 5 slots left. The devs will not budge on this because they love the idea of pants being put on making you able to carry less.
Tools also need to be repaired, but you can do this for free. The only reason they need to repair is so that you keep running back to base to do it and you keep wasting your time. This game has a lot of padded out time in it from when the game had less content that remains. 
Anyway, The mining thing takes a long time because you need to mine it, carry it back in small amounts to not be over weighted, wait for it to smelt, and then carry it back to chests (this is more annoying than it sounds because of how little metal you can carry). Default settings make it so you can't even portal metal. Every thing in this game  is made to pad out runtime. After the second biome the next two are just the same thing with a different coat of paint but more insufferable for reasons I will get into later.

Lets talk about the combat and stamina system.
This is the worst part for me and the two are deeply intertwined. Stamina regenerates slowly in this game and is used by pretty much every action other than plain walking. Want to swing any tool or weapon? stamina. Dodge? stamina. Run or jump? stamina. And it is not a small amount for any of these. Stamina needs you to have a rested bonus to regenerate faster than a disabled snail. Rested bonus is your reward for making a cozy base and it is kind of ok but it is just a cozy way of the game making you run back to base every twenty minutes at minimum. Rested bonus has an evil twin though, wet debuff is terrible and often unavoidable. No matter what you do when you are wet you will always be barely scraping by on stamina. This is a major problem because one biome is the SWAMP where it rains 24/7. 
Ok time for some combat talk now that I have introduced stamina. This combat feels like if someone looked at soulslike combat and thought "huh, what if I made it worse in literally every way?" Every enemy feels like it attacks in slow motion but they hit like a truck. If you dodge but get caught on terrain I hope you made peace with any god you worship if you do that. Combat often evolves into walking away from the enemy you are fighting as you regain stamina before going back in to whittle down the often very tanky enemies. You can eat food that gives a lot of stamina instead of health but it does not increase the rate it regains. No matter what in a short amount of time you will end up waiting for stamina to regenerate regardless of if you ate stamina food or health food. (I think I forgot how apain it can be to get the food but trust me, it is a whole grind so you can do another grind without being oneshot by a fodder enemy. I actually got two shot by a mosquito once). The game also has an awful parry system that really does not work well. It gives you an extremely long window to parry after you put your shield up but you have to have a bunch of total HP if you want to not get one shot by the enemy you are attempting to parry. I am not kidding. If you don't have a magic amount of total HP the game does not tell you that varies between enemies and their attacks different attacks a parry just ends up being a hit on you. Parrying even when done right drains so much stamina that often you can't even punish them for getting parried. A parry also launches the enemy away from you, making it even harder to capitalize on the parry. Even with all I just said parries are by far the least insufferable way to do combat.
I did not even talk about how the swing animations can't be tilted up or down. This makes combat on a slight hill practically unplayable until you lure them onto a flatter area. This would be forgivable if one of the biomes was not THE ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ MOUNTIANS. I mean, genuinely what the hell were they thinking? These cliffs are insanely steep. You pretty much need to just shoot everything with bows or throw spears to kill anything safely. Combat is just so unapproachable in this area that I never really did any of it unless completely forced. 
You also need to collect 200lb eggs on this mountain to summon the boss so that is fun. That is by far the majority of the weight you can carry in one item that you need to collect a bunch of then bring to the top of a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ MOUNTIAN.
So yeah I always get burnt out at the mountians. I also forgot that the boss fights can be so far away from spawn that you end up going through way later game areas to get to it and end up oneshot by some fodder like a mosquito or something. All the bosses and mini bosses are such tanks that you will be fighting them for 20+ minutes sometimes. Me and my friends end up cheesing them often not because they are hard but because it is just so much less mentally exhausting to cheese an enemy than to walk way from it for most of the 20 minutes as you regain stamina.
This game is just not fun and is 100% set on padding out runtime. I didn't even get into half of the grind because this review has been mentally exhausting to write kind of like this game is to play. The game is 50% hitting rocks, 20% walking way from enemies while out of stamina, 10% waiting at base for something/farming, 10% fighting, 10% building, and 10% of whatever else the game has. No part is really enjoyable other than some of the views in game.

Stuck in Dev hell with Devs that add useless bloat and focus more on their plushies then updates to the game,  Game is fun no doubt but Will never give a positive review to greedy devs who can't do their job lmao.

This game is another reason why we need a 'maybe' option for steam reviews. It can be very difficult at the start and frustrating learning the game solo, but I can definetley recommend playing the game with friends starting fresh and just take your time to learn the game mecanics if you have played previously and didnt enjoy it. Sometimes i just spend time building and not worrying about progression or i explore as the world is absolutley massive! Its one of those games were you can appretiate the amount of quality work has been put into making the world feel alive and breathing as you survive amd make it your own, and that takes alot of time and patience, just like real life.

It's gameplay is similar to project zomboid. In the early game you spend your time looting, staying out of fights, and gathering tools. Eventually you can battle stronger foes for more loot. It has a skill system where upon death you lose levels in your strongest skills. But to balance it for about 15 minutes you can die without skill loss, and when you gather your loot back you get faster and lose less stamina. Its food system is unique where you can make a meal with 3 different types of food that gives health and stamina for a amount of time. When you sleep your character may have a dream thats selected out of a table of dreams. The character creation is both good and bad: its very fitting for its setting but the lighting makes it hard to select the right color. The art style is also very similar to project zomboid with low poly models, and smaller textures. Its base building is also very interesting as you need a set of workbenchs and to level them up you need addons. Meaning you will need to have some good layout planning to fit everything. Just dont put a fireplace without a chimney. Over all its a great game that has settings to allow even the weakest computers run it.

Best survival open world game out there. Not one of the best, but the best.

Valheim has also paved the way for a lot of newer survival games, inspiring mechanics and systems that other studios have clearly taken influence from. Just look at games like Enshrouded and Windrose with features such as the “Comfort Level” system, roof-building mechanics, angled roof pieces, and more.

This is the survival game. I’m 292 hours in and still haven’t even reached Ashlands, the current latest biome. I’ve played pretty much every survival game you can think of, starting all the way back with Minecraft when I was younger, and I’m genuinely glad Iron Gate AB created something this incredible for people to experience.

Valheim has everything. Coziness, intensity, humor, fear, exploration, and unforgettable moments. Very few games manage to balance all of those feelings as perfectly as this one does.

I... came back from 5 years away. God, I forgot how unpolished so many of the game mechanics are. How much of a snooze fest the traversal is. How there are almost no QOL features. How bad the combat is due to no stamina. How bad the building system is. When you compare this game, to any other games in the genre, you realize it does nothing the best.... at all.... It's actually sad because when it came out, I had such rose tinted glasses on from everyone hyping me up. But now that there is no hype, and I've come back, and let all of the thoughts about how much time is wasted through arbitrary means, and the lack of QOL features that I figured they had put into the game by now.... man it was kind of a slap in the face when I realized, oh.... this game isn't one of the greats is it. At every single turn, there are bad decisions over and over. The game doesn't respect your time at all. You constantly are redoing the same things over and over because they wanted to waste your time as much as they could. Like, have you actually looked at all of the little things this game does, to just f you over. You have to burn wood in gd torches in the wall for christ sakes in order to see in your base. Everything has a durability. Everything needs a roof. I'm surprised they didn't spoil food, I guess thats the one caviat they haven't thought of yet too make you spend even more time doing menial crap for no reason. Also, hey I know you probably spent 50+ hours making a beautiful base, that you love coming back to. Oh, ya create a warehouse sized square shed that looks like trash to put these idiot wisps in. GG. Go f yourself devs.

Back when this game came out, I couldn't get enough of this game, and I loved it, would recommend it to anyone. But now that time has passed, the hype is gone, and the rose tinted glasses have come off. No, this game is fkin trash because it doesn't respect your time, and wants to spit in your face. Hey but I guess there is a positive, you can get hundreds of frames because it looks like crap, and you can't see anything anymore due to fog/mist/nighttime so.... hurray I guess for that.
Fun game, survival aspects are not too harsh, combat is decent and building is fun. The skill loss on death is not my favourite, but especially in co-op this game can be a lot of fun.
I loved this game my first play through, with friends, but coming back after other games (like Windrose) just highlights how god-awful this game is.  I would type more, but I'm out of stamina

First wicked good game.
Now for the huge BUT. The lack of inventory options quickly devalues the game. Once you get going the "inventory full" message becoms all too annoying and familiar.

I will be installing an inventory mode to make the game playable. I can only assume the developer(s) wanted to add a pointlessly low inventory cap to annoy players. Honestly may not have started the game If I had known the inventory cap. 

It is really sad as this is an absolutely stunningly fun world building game. But the lack of inventory space I want to say destroys the fun. Real life is hard enough. I shouldn't have to manage inventory this bad in the game.

I cannot recommend the game until they increase the vanilla inventories size.
I dont see what all the commotion is about. this game leaves a lot to be desired. just seems kinda bleh for this genre. Plenty other games do this better than valheim does.
I found the combat was like a worse world of warcraft but singleplayer and that the grind made me wanting for other games.   I'm sure this game has its appeal but not with me.

I really want to like this game the way I did when I tried it out for the first time, but subsequent playthroughs made me realise I'm playing a cheap MMO-like.
My biggest gripes with it:
1. Tedious and repetitive progression system (farm galore), every run feels the same.
2. Simple QoL features like using materials from containers are still not implemented. Let me remind you game was released in 2021.
3. No user-friendly way to manage your save files. Each autosave backup is 2 hours apart. There are server related bugs that will make you lose a lot of your progress.

the game is cool and all, but the farming is insane, the progression is a joke and you get the magic when you completed the world, and for gods sake the stamina, I HATE THE STAMINA SYSTEM, you cant also get a big base, because the game is programmed with the feet, you get intense lag if you make a cool looking house, they show you great castles, bases and cool houses, but man, the game struggles with a simple box house, you cant play with 4 friends and u, because the game dies. the particles sucks also, if you place anything theres a dust particle that will give you lag if you dare to make a quick construccion, the dust will never desapear, combat feels awful also,  this intired game is a big experience for frustuation, its been on Early Access Game for 5 years and im sure they will leave the game like that another ten years.

Surprisingly deep, yet simple. Excellent building system. Many different ways to play.

Although this game has it's issues at the moment and the updates take a while this has by far been my favorite game. this game finds a way to balance the elemants of a calm game with a difficult game in most biomes (apart from the last one which is just brutal). I played with my father and my friends and had to most fun in this game with them. This game is great solo but a challenage and even better with friends. The ONLY issue I have is this is a long game it can take forever to do stuff and sometimes feel like a drag but in the end it is all worth it. 1.0 is just on the horizon and I am glad to see it come after 5 years of work from the devs. I hope that the final update is just as good as the last few because I really want to build in the deep north.

You can alternate between sessions of building, crafting, exploring, fiighting and crafting for ressources. 
The build lets you get very creative and accomplish beautiful things. 
Love, love love it

[h1]Valheim[/h1]

[i]A simple-looking game with far more soul than most games twice its size.[/i]

[hr][/hr]

Valheim is one of my favourite games. I have been playing it pretty much since launch, back when the game had bugs absolutely everywhere, and I still play it today.

Now we have more content, more story, more things to do... and still a few bugs, because Valheim is still Valheim.

[h2]The world, the music and the atmosphere[/h2]

The graphics are simple, but beautiful in their own way. It does not try to look realistic, and I actually think that is part of its charm.

The music is also really good. It is calm, relaxing, and fits perfectly with that feeling of exploring a strange world where something will probably try to kill you in the next five minutes.

Exploration is one of the best parts of the game. The biomes are interesting and always make you want to see what is beyond the next hill, across the sea, or inside that very suspicious-looking forest.

[h2]Enemies and biomes[/h2]

In my opinion, each biome could have a bit more monster variety, but the enemies that are there do have personality.

It does not feel like the lazy “same monster, but now it is red instead of blue” kind of design. Each creature feels like it belongs where it is.

[h2]Building is where Valheim really shines[/h2]

The building system is excellent. Building a base, a house, a harbour, a wall, or even an entire village is genuinely fun.

The fact that you can also shape the terrain makes everything more interesting and gives you a lot of room to be creative. If you enjoy building and decorating, Valheim does that part very well.

[h2]Mods make it even better[/h2]

Another strong point is the modding community. The game is already good without mods, but with them it becomes even better.

You can improve quality of life, change some mechanics, make certain things less painful, or adapt the gameplay to your own style.

Basically, you can fix some of the things the developers apparently left there for character building.

[h2]The two real weak points[/h2]

[list]
[*][b]The developers should give the game more love.[/b]

Valheim is a great game, but sometimes it feels like it lacks care, pace and proper evolution. The foundation is excellent. It just needs to be treated with a bit more affection and consistency.

[*][b]The game should be much more popular.[/b]

Valheim has huge creative potential, almost like what Minecraft had in terms of freedom to build, explore and create your own stories.

I said [i]almost[/i], of course, because Minecraft is its own ridiculous monster, but Valheim deserves far more attention than it gets.
[/list]

[h2]Best played with friends[/h2]

I also do not think Valheim is at its best when played alone. You can play solo, of course, but for me the game truly shines with friends.

Exploring, building, dying in incredibly stupid ways, getting lost at sea, being bullied by a new biome... all of that is much better when someone else is there to laugh at your suffering.

[hr][/hr]

[h2]Final thoughts[/h2]

Overall, Valheim is a simple-looking game with a lot of soul.

If you enjoy survival, exploration, building and co-op gameplay, it is very easy to recommend.

Pretty fun so far. Combat and game systems are good.

Alright folks... Buckle up cuz Imma review the living schaboinks out of this game...
I have played this game with my friends for a looong time and we have went thru a decent number of changes in the game. This game can be a lot of fun, it has so many things to do that sometimes you forget the goal of the game. It has some problems tho that can completely stop you from having fun. There are still issues with the game that can lead to some unfair deaths or progress halts and that can be really frustrating. Yea yea the game is in Early Access blah blah bla... FOR 5 F***ING YEARS? There are still issues in this game that have been here since 2021. The Devs keep adding stuff that just piles up problems. Amazing game!

Valheim is already a fantastic experience on its own, but if the grind ever starts to feel tedious, or you find yourself losing a bit of motivation, mods can breathe a whole new life into the game.

I’ve been playing with Creature Level and Loot Control, Epic Loot, Warfare: Fire and Ice, Monstrum, Armory, Wizardry, Monstrum: Deep North, Warfare, and EpicLoot_Therzie. Together, these mods complement each other really well and add a ton of new content, enemies, loot, progression, and variety to the game. Some of them also help everything work more smoothly together, making the whole experience feel surprisingly cohesive.

I’d also highly recommend quality-of-life mods like TeleportEverything, Backpacks, AzuAutoStore, AzuExtendedPlayerInventory, AzuCraftyBoxes, and AreaRepair. These make the day-to-day gameplay so much smoother. You can sort your chests once, then later just toss items on the ground and auto-store them. Need to craft something? As long as your storage is nearby, you can craft directly using materials from your boxes. It saves a huge amount of time and cuts down on the grind, letting you focus more on exploring, building, fighting, and having fun.

10/10 game. 11/10 with mods.

I have played many many games in the open world survival crafting genre.  This game stands above all of the others in my opinion.  I enjoy it far more and keep coming back to play it again.  

What is tragic is I can't identify what Valheim does differently from all those others that makes it better.  Some combination of the gameplay loop, art design, and music I suppose.  It gives the world a very unique vibe.

My only complaint about this game is that I enjoy it much more using options that are only available through mods like Valheim Plus.  It would be very nice if the developer would include those kinds of configurations in the base game, so we don't have to play the game of updating mods and getting them updated on everyone else's machine!  A real PITA when you are playing with a group that are not all comfortable with downloading and installing mods.  The biggest thing that Valheim Plus provides is a means of "tuning the grind".  All of these games ultimately come down to "gather resource and use resource" and Valheim Plus lets you configure how much time the "gather resource" step takes.  I appreciate that now that I no longer have as much time to dedicate to games as I did when I was younger.

One of my favorite games of all time! lore is beautiful, scenery is stunning and the MUSIC! I could go on forever about all of the things I love about this game, and I will continue to play it for years to come.

The only downside about this game is lack of npcs because holy moly do I get fking lonely, I really quit and play again because of the equally lack of npc of people in real life, so it's an endless cycle. No, i dont have to see a psychiatrist because thats what chagpt is for, so stop asking

Stung by bees, boars harassing me, my shed looks like ♥♥♥♥, got my ass handed to me by a spooky skeleton, a bear ate me in one go and finally a giant ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ troll deleted me in one hit. 10/10 game, sometimes frustrating but it's beautiful and engaging. Everytime I die I feel like I have an obstacle to overcome and gotta figure it out. It's easy to go step by step, evolve and beat the bastards that killed me.

It's about 7/10 I'd say. If Steam had a three tier rating system, I'd probably give it mediocre instead of "Thumbs up".

The survival and building mechanics are great. 
It can be super fun to build your little viking outpost or stone castles or whatever. There are also (togglable) raids to give you a bit of reason to build them to last. Also, I enjoy the combat. It's seems to have that Dark Souls kinda heft to it; It's punishing (depending on difficulty), it has quite a bit of animation priority so you need to gauge the tiiming of your attacks and blocks. But it's not that complicated either.

I personally enjoy the core gameplay loop quite a bit; Enter a biome, explore and exploit it for resources. Craft better equiptment to take on the boss of the biome. After that, enter new biome and do it all over again, with different crafting stuff unlocking there.

I also find the graphics passable, at times even endearing. Some environments/3d models have a bit of that "Classic WoW" magic in their simplistic artstyle. Though, it's a mixed bag. There's not alot of variety in any given biome, artistically. I'd wish for a bit more tree and rock models, for example... Also, a few assets in the game look downright ugly to my eye, the giant yggdrasil overlooking you whereever you go, for example.

My single biggest point of criticism, and it's a big one, is frankly the overall lack of variety in things to see and do.

Like I said, I like the core building and survival aspects. But the world has very little variety in the things you find and do in it. Once you've explored ~5 minutes of any given biome, you've seen like 90% of what it has to offer. It's just repeated ad nauseam. Naturally some of this is due to procedurally generated world but that certainly doesn't excuse it, since so many games do quite wounderful things with it.

And the fact that the game is nearing version 1.0, I'm unfortunately not hopeful that that main point of criticism will see much addressing... What with the dev team focusing on polishing the end-game biomes. Though I'd be very happy to be proven wrong!

So overall, I'm pretty happy with the game; It's resource gathering - crafting system loop has a hold on me. But I'm constantly hoping that there'd be MORE to everything...

Valheim is an absolute masterpiece in the survival genre. I've sunk over 100 hours into this game and I still keep coming back to it.
The game throws you into a beautiful, mysterious Viking afterlife where you have to survive, build, explore, and defeat ancient bosses to prove your worth to Odin. What makes Valheim special is how well everything comes together.

A survival game with a pleasing visual style set in a Norse Mythology setting—what's not to love? I've been having a lot of fun in Valheim, and a lot of the game's distinctive mechanics are really fun.

In particular, I like that your maximum health and stamina are determined by what food you've eaten recently—genius! I don't think I've seen this in a survival game before, and it makes food a lot more important to the game loop. You'll spend quite a bit of your time hunting for food, and [spoiler]you'll want to craft a spear and a bow ASAP, since those make hunting boars and especially deer a whole lot easier. For hunting [i]necks[/i], though, a knife should suffice.[/spoiler]

The skill progression resembles that of the Basic RolePlaying ruleset, for you fellow TTRPG types out there. You improve your skills through practice, which is a system I've always quite liked in games. (for those of you who don't play tabletop RPGs, it's kind of like Pirates of the Caribbean Online or Elder Scrolls: Oblivion)

The building controls took a bit of time to get the hang of, but building is a lot of fun once you figure it out.

One last thing to remember if you're playing with a controller: pressing the Left Button and Left Stick at the same time toggles Auto-Pickup for items on the ground. If you stop being able to pick things up automatically, that's why. Just toggle it back on, and you're good!
It's not a super satisfying experience but the gameplay loop is fun enough with a duo. Progressing is miserable but it's a part of the fun.

its an excellent game. unpolished but I think given time, it'll develop into something very rustic and great! its difficult single player and theres noticable difficulty spikes after the first boss. its clearly a game designed to be played with multiple people as opposed to alone with the way progression and some things such as boss fights, particular dungeons, and hunting sea serpents works (its easier if someone has a harpoon while you steer) however I belive the game has potential. fix the progression, fill in the gaps in the equipment and make building less cancerous (even with a hoe its still iffy) and it'd be almost perfect.

the norse theme isnt my favorite but thats subjective and more of a taste thing then a game thing. I've also had some compatability issues getting Steam's Valheim to mesh with Vortex Mods (Via Nexus Website, as it says I dont own the game even when I do and am logged in) however I've been using r2modman. it isnt perfect but its the best workaround I could find. this could be an isolated incident or an issue on my end but I feel everything is worth sharing in something like this in case it is a bug or other people are having the same experences.
Excellent game, hope the content continues for many years!
Its a nice game to play with your friends and i love the survival side of it and reminds me the old mmorpgs i used to play
great game to play with friends, looking forward to future updates!

Stop looking at the screenshots and thinking this is just another generic Viking crafting game. Valheim is actually a masterpiece of psychological pacing that strips away the modern gaming power fantasy and forces you to survive a brutal world that completely ignores your existence. The genius of this game lies in the terrifying contrast between how hostile the world is outside and how incredibly safe it feels inside. While big budget titles use photorealism to flex tech Valheim uses low poly models paired with god tier lighting and wind physics to create a suffocating atmosphere you can actually feel. When a storm hits the Black Forest the canopy bends violently and the fog cuts your vision making you feel completely small and exposed. This overwhelming dread is why building is the real endgame. Your base is not some cute cosmetic project for looks because if you do not understand structural engineering the gravity and physics will punish you harder than Radahn from Elden Ring. If you do not support your roof beams properly the entire build will crumble over your head and if you forget to vent the smoke you will literally suffocate on your own campfire. We do not build because a tutorial told us to but because the game forces us to crave actual functional shelter just to survive the night.

This intense loop only works because the game completely refuses to respect your time by banning metal through portals. By forcing you to physically sail your ore across the map Valheim brings back the high stakes expedition that modern gaming killed with fast travel. When you load up a longship you are risking hours of real life labor because the sea swells can capsize your livelihood and a single ocean serpent will trigger genuine primal panic. The map feels massive because the distance is allowed to matter turning a basic resource run into an absolute odyssey. Ultimately Valheim is a giant lesson in humility where a rolling log can end your playthrough yet because the danger is so heavy surviving feels better than beating any cinematic boss fight. Every death is a fair story of your own greed or lack of preparation and that turns those desperate naked corpse runs on a wooden raft into the best memories you will have of the game. It is not about conquering purgatory. It is about finding a place to belong inside it. This is a ten out of ten and I will definitely get crushed by a birch tree again.
_____________________________________________________

TLDR

Valheim is a psychological horror game disguised as a survival adventure that weaponizes the weather to make you feel pathetic. Your base is a literal lifeline where gravity physics will demolish your work like a boss fight if you build it wrong. It is the only game on Steam that understands a properly vented smoking chimney is more important than a legendary sword because it is the only thing standing between you and a world that truly wants you dead. You will spend ten hours building a house not because you want to but because the outside world is so genuinely terrifying that you will do anything just to feel safe for five minutes. If you want a game that respects your intelligence by absolutely disrespecting your time and your safety then buy this immediately.

Also just in case nobody has told you lately I am incredibly proud of you for making it this far in your life. I am not going to pretend to know the specific storms you have had to sail through or the weight of the armor you carry every single day. But in a world that can feel just as relentless and indifferent as this game simply waking up and finding the strength to push through to the next sunrise is a victory. If you are out here navigating the chaos and refusing to let the heavy days break you then you are already a modern day viking. Look back at everything you have survived so far and realize you have earned the right to be proud of yourself. Keep building your hearth and keep moving forward.

Just make sure to look out for them birch trees because just like in our Valheim world they will absolutely ♥♥♥♥ you up in real life too.
